topic.title_cn: 向非专业人士解释复杂概念
topic.prompt_en: Describe how you explain a complex concept to someone without technical knowledge.
topic.learning_goal: Learn to simplify a complex concept without making it misleading or childish.

angles.title: Set the work context
content: Say what the work situation is and what your role is.
That gives the listener a frame before the details begin.

angles.title: Explain the issue or task
content: Describe the task, bug, delay, or concept in practical terms.
Avoid jargon if the topic is about clear communication.

angles.title: Clarify responsibility
content: Show what belongs to you, to the team, or to the process.
That keeps the answer professional instead of defensive.

angles.title: End with the next step
content: Finish with what you did or plan to do next.
A work answer should feel forward-looking.

sequence.target: State the role, project, or workplace situation clearly.
Explain what happened and how you handled it.
End with responsibility, learning, or action.

high_score_tips: Workplace answers sound stronger when they focus on clarity, not self-praise.
Explain the practical effect of a problem, not just the technical label.
Responsibility language should sound calm and specific.
End with the next step or lesson so the answer feels professional.

content.why_it_hurts: A workplace answer loses impact if the listener cannot understand the main point.
content.fix: Translate the issue into plain effect, such as delay, confusion, or customer impact.

content.why_it_hurts: The answer sounds weaker if you only try to avoid blame.
content.fix: Explain responsibility honestly and move quickly to what you did next.

content.why_it_hurts: Work answers feel incomplete if the story stops at the problem.
content.fix: End with the next step, fix, or communication follow-up.

content.why_it_hurts: The response sounds memorised if it stays at the level of general ideas and never lands on one lived detail.
content.fix: Add one concrete moment, reaction, or small consequence that actually happened to you.

content.problem: Reflection that only repeats the opening
content.why_it_hurts: The ending feels flat if it only restates the topic instead of adding insight or change.
content.fix: Use the final lines to show what changed, what you realised, or how you would respond now.

language.reason: Happen is intransitive and does not take passive form.

language.better_example: I explained the problem to him.
language.reason: Explain usually takes the thing before the person with to.

language.problem: Overusing very or really with basic adjectives
language.wrong_example: It was very good and really important for me.
language.better_example: It turned out to be more meaningful than I expected.
language.reason: A precise evaluation sounds more natural than stacking intensifiers on basic adjectives.

language.problem: Repeating I think in every sentence
language.wrong_example: I think it is useful, and I think it matters because I think it affects daily life.
language.better_example: What makes it useful to me is the way it affects daily life.
language.reason: Removing repeated I think makes the answer sound more confident and more fluent.

core.example: I would naturally use "role" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "deadline" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "issue" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "update" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "team" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "responsibility" when talking about this topic.

extended.note: Useful for bug explanations and practical analysis.
Natural for progress and delay reports.
Useful when translating a work issue into plain language.

core.example: I would use "I am responsible for" naturally in this answer.
I would use "at the moment" naturally in this answer.
I would use "the main issue is" naturally in this answer.
I would use "the next step is" naturally in this answer.
I would use "to put it simply" naturally in this answer.
I would use "take responsibility for" naturally in this answer.

extended.phrase: from a practical point of view
extended.example: I would use "from a practical point of view" when making the answer sound more natural.
I would use "what I did next was" when making the answer sound more natural.

extended.phrase: the impact on the team was
extended.example: I would use "the impact on the team was" when making the answer sound more natural.

extended.phrase: I wanted to make it clear that
extended.example: I would use "I wanted to make it clear that" when making the answer sound more natural.

framework.goal: Handle workplace topics through context, issue, responsibility, and next step.

moves: Set the work context and your role.
Explain the issue, update, or concept in practical language.
State how you responded or communicated.
Clarify ownership and the next action.
Keep the ending constructive and professional.

samples.answer:
The way i explain something complex to people outside the field is something I can talk about quite naturally. When I explain a complex concept to someone without technical experience, I start with the purpose instead of the mechanics. For example, if I were explaining an API, I would not begin with formal definitions. I would say it is a way for two systems to exchange information in a structured manner. Once the basic picture is clear, I might use one simple analogy and then return to the real situation so the explanation does not stay too abstract. The challenge is keeping it accurate while removing unnecessary detail.

What made it worth talking about was not only the event or idea itself, but also the effect it had on the way I think or act. If the other person understands the purpose and the main limitation, the explanation has probably done its job. Overall, it is a topic that feels real to me because it connects to ordinary life rather than a perfect story.
highlights: Band 6 answer matched to the 向非专业人士解释复杂概念 topic
Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

band_guide.focus: Clear work context and next step
opening: The topic that comes to mind is the way I explain something complex to people outside the field.
I would first explain the background clearly.
body: Then I would add the central detail or turning point.
After that, I would show the result or practical effect.
closing: Finally, I would end with the takeaway or present view.

upgrades.improved: If the other person understands the purpose and the main limitation, the explanation has probably done its job.
upgrades.why: A specific reason sounds much more natural than a general claim.
upgrades.note: Use the personal effect to close the answer.

upgrades.improved: Once the basic picture is clear, I might use one simple analogy and then return to the real situation so the explanation does not stay too abstract.
upgrades.why: A clear turning point gives the answer shape and movement.
upgrades.note: This is especially useful in spoken storytelling and reflective answers.

upgrades.original: It was very important to me.
upgrades.improved: What made it important was the effect it had on the way I handled similar situations later.
upgrades.why: This explains importance through a concrete effect instead of a vague adjective.
upgrades.note: Useful when you want a simple but natural reflective sentence.

upgrades.original: I learned a lot from it.
upgrades.improved: I came away from it with a clearer idea of what I would do next time.
upgrades.why: This sounds more spoken and more specific than a flat learned a lot.
upgrades.note: Good for endings that focus on takeaway and future behaviour.

samples.answer:
The topic that comes to mind for me is the way I explain something complex to people outside the field. When I explain a complex concept to someone without technical experience, I start with the purpose instead of the mechanics. For example, if I were explaining an API, I would not begin with formal definitions. I would say it is a way for two systems to exchange information in a structured manner. What stood out most was that once the basic picture is clear, I might use one simple analogy and then return to the real situation so the explanation does not stay too abstract.

After that, the challenge is keeping it accurate while removing unnecessary detail. What I find interesting about it now is that the situation seemed fairly ordinary at the time, but it revealed something more lasting about how I handle pressure, choices, or relationships. If the other person understands the purpose and the main limitation, the explanation has probably done its job. So if I were explaining this in a speaking test, I would focus less on sounding dramatic and more on why the experience or idea genuinely stayed with me.
highlights: Band 7 answer matched to the 向非专业人士解释复杂概念 topic
Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

body: Then I would add the central detail or turning point.

closing: Finally, I would end with the takeaway or present view.

upgrades.improved: If the other person understands the purpose and the main limitation, the explanation has probably done its job.

upgrades.note: Use the personal effect to close the answer.

upgrades.improved: Once the basic picture is clear, I might use one simple analogy and then return to the real situation so the explanation does not stay too abstract.
upgrades.why: A clear turning point gives the answer shape and movement.
upgrades.note: This is especially useful in spoken storytelling and reflective answers.

upgrades.improved: What helped most was that it changed the way I judged the situation in practice.
upgrades.why: This sounds more mature and more natural than the vague phrase helped me a lot.
upgrades.note: Useful when you want to show growth without sounding dramatic.

upgrades.improved: It gave me a more balanced way to look at similar situations afterwards.
upgrades.why: Balanced way to look at is a more idiomatic reflective phrase in spoken English.
upgrades.note: Works well in opinion, process, and experience answers.

samples.answer:
If I had to answer this topic in a more reflective way, I would still begin with the way I explain something complex to people outside the field. When I explain a complex concept to someone without technical experience, I start with the purpose instead of the mechanics. For example, if I were explaining an API, I would not begin with formal definitions. I would say it is a way for two systems to exchange information in a structured manner. The turning point, for me, was that once the basic picture is clear, I might use one simple analogy and then return to the real situation so the explanation does not stay too abstract.

From there, the challenge is keeping it accurate while removing unnecessary detail. What matters now is not the surface event or statement alone, but the shift in perspective behind it. If the other person understands the purpose and the main limitation, the explanation has probably done its job. In that sense, the topic feels useful because it shows not only what happened or what I think, but also how my judgment has become a little more precise over time.

Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

upgrades.note: This is especially useful in spoken storytelling and reflective answers.

upgrades.note: Strong for higher-band closings that need subtlety rather than drama.

upgrades.improved: Its real value lay in the way it quietly reshaped my response patterns over time.
upgrades.why: Quietly reshaped and over time make the reflection sound more precise and more natural.

practice.task: Describe how you explain a complex concept to someone without technical knowledge.
checklist: State the work context and your role.
Explain the issue or concept in clear language.
End with ownership and a next step.
self_prompts: What was the actual issue or task?
